data.title: Ex-aide of Malaysia’s Anwar to stand trial on corruption charges linked to businessman
data.description:
A former political aide of Malaysia’s leader and a Sabah-linked businessman pleaded not guilty to four counts of corruption each on Thursday, in a case analysts say will taint Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim’s anti-corruption credentials.
Anwar’s reform platform has already been tested by disputes over mineral exploration rights in Malaysia’s poorest but most resource-rich state, Sabah.
Former political secretary Shamsul Iskandar Mohd Akin was accused of receiving 176,829.03 ringgit (US$42,930) in cash and gifts comprising furniture and electrical products from businessman Albert Tei between November 24, 2023 and March 5, 2024, allegedly as inducement to help companies linked to Tei secure approval for mineral exploration licences in Sabah, the Kuala Lumpur Sessions Court was told.
After all four charges were read out to Shamsul, 51, who was standing in the dock in a green batik shirt, he said: “I plead not guilty, and claim trial”.
If convicted, Shamsul and Tei each face up to 20 years’ jail and a fine of at least five times the value of the bribe or 10,000 ringgit, whichever is higher.
Bail for Shamsul was set at 150,000 ringgit with two Malaysian sureties, while Tei is out on bail of 70,000 ringgit. Both were ordered to surrender their passports, report to the anti-corruption authorities once a month, and not interfere with witnesses.
The case will be mentioned again on January 8.

Outside the court, Shamsul told reporters that he was confident of clearing his name.
“[God willing], I’ll prove my innocence. We will follow the legal process in court,” he said. “From the very beginning … I have taken a proactive approach and cooperated fully with the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission to facilitate the investigation, and I’ll clear my name. I hope the truth will prevail.”
The charges follow days of scrutiny over claims that Tei had funded property renovations, cigars and tailored suits for Anwar’s former aide, along with screenshots of WhatsApp messages that appeared to show Shamsul requesting foreign currency before travelling abroad.
Before his current case, Tei faced separate charges for allegedly offering bribes connected to a mining licence, involving two Sabah state assemblymen. He was charged as the person offering the payments despite being the initial whistle-blower in that episode.
Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission chief commissioner Azam Baki said on Wednesday that the agency had completed its investigation after interviewing 32 witnesses.
Scrutiny around Shamsul grew earlier after a support letter he signed last year surfaced online, urging the health ministry to consider six contractors for a Johor hospital project. Opposition lawmakers questioned why Anwar did not remove him, saying it undercut the government’s anti-corruption stance.
Anwar, who came to power pledging to clean up Malaysia’s corruption culture, insisted on Tuesday he would not intervene even if investigations were to implicate his allies.


“I told them they were free to investigate … We will not give protection to any party [involved in corruption],” he told parliament.
The prime minister has also fielded questions about another former aide, Farhash Wafa Salvador, who was accused in July of securing exclusive exploration rights over 70,000 hectares (172,973 acres) in Sabah.
State authorities said no approvals were issued, and the MACC cleared Farhash in September.
‘Big blow for Anwar’
Analysts say the latest case has eroded trust at a moment when Anwar can least afford it. His Pakatan Harapan coalition suffered a bruising weekend in Sabah’s election, winning only one of 73 state seats.
According to Ahmad Mohsein Azman, a senior analyst at BowerGroupAsia, the Shamsul affair has cut deeper than previous controversies.
“Prime Minister Anwar’s anti-corruption credentials will undoubtedly be impacted by this scandal,” he told This Week in Asia.
Shamsul’s case was the “biggest fissure” to have negatively affected Anwar, although its revelation was “more a question of when than if”, as others close to him were also alleged to have been involved in shady dealings since the prime minister took office, Mohsein said.
“It marks the first time in Anwar’s premiership that his close aide is to be charged. This will be a big blow for him, given how his ‘Reformasi’ life work had been fixated on combating corruption in the first place.”

data.title: 16 students who lost homes in Hong Kong fire get financial support, school supplies
data.description:
Sixteen Hong Kong students from a secondary school opposite the scene of a deadly blaze in Tai Po have received financial support, new textbooks and uniforms after losing their homes to the fire, their principal has said.
Cheng Sze-wang, head of Wong Shiu Chi Secondary School, also said on Thursday that the students were temporarily staying with their relatives or in other homes within the district.
Students in need were also offered counselling, while all dictations and assessments had been suspended to avoid putting extra pressure on them, he added.
The school is just a 10-minute walk away from Wang Fuk Court, the scene of an inferno last week that claimed at least 159 lives. Many of the school’s classroom windows directly face the estate.
“Most of [the affected students] are now living in their friends’ and relatives’ homes, and prefer living in the district as they find transitional housing [in other places] too far away for them,” Cheng said.
“Some parents have already rented private housing for a temporary stay. [The students] now go to school as normal and have no big problem.”
He added that vendors had moved quickly to resolve the loss of students’ textbooks and uniforms to the fire, while families had received financial support from authorities and NGOs on Monday.




School management, staff and alumni also came together to raise funds for the affected students, with the youngsters to receive the first round of donations this week.
At least 13 schools in Tai Po suspended classes on Thursday last week after the fire broke out the day before.
Cheng said his school had called parents one by one to understand each student’s emotional needs when classes resumed the day after.
“Most of the students in class had watched the fire rage as [many of] our classroom windows directly face Wang Fuk Court,” he said.
“Students had different levels of emotional needs, and some told us or wrote about their feelings. Our teachers and social workers counselled them on Monday.”
The school had already asked teachers to suspend dictations and tests as the fire had affected many students emotionally, impacting their studies, Cheng said.
“If there are any dictation and tests they have to handle, it will put more pressure on them,” he said.

data.title: Bag, pen, workwear propel Takaichi to style icon status in Japan
data.description:
The pledge by Japan’s Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi to “work, work, work, work and work” for her country has been named the catchphrase of the year.
The ultraconservative Takaichi uttered the phrase in October when she was elected as head of the ruling Liberal Democratic Party. Many were initially as worried about her work ethic as supportive of her enthusiasm.
Accepting her award from a private committee this week, Takaichi said she only wanted to stress her enthusiasm and that her comments had been somewhat misinterpreted.
“I have no intention of encouraging other people to overwork or suggesting long working hours as a virtue,” Takaichi said. “I hope there is no misunderstanding.”
Since taking office in late October as the nation’s first female prime minister, Takaichi has also captured public attention for her fashion, with women scrambling to copy her style.
But while her dress sense has won admiration from younger women who say they are “Sana-katsu”, or rooting for Sanae, it is unclear if her hardline conservative policies will win the same praise.
Takaichi is looking to regain right-wing supporters after the LDP’s big election losses under her moderate predecessor Shigeru Ishiba.
During her speech to party members on October 4, she promised an all-out effort to rebuild the struggling party and regain public support, urging lawmakers to “work like a horse”. Then she added: “I will abandon the idea of a ‘work-life balance’ – I will work, work, work, work and work.”
Repeating the word “work” in a low, determined voice left a strong impression at the time.

Takaichi’s apparent long working hours and lack of sleep have worried fellow lawmakers. She held a meeting with aides at 3am before the first day of parliament on November 7, though she has not started that early since.
“I sleep about two hours now, four hours at the longest,” Takaichi, who also provides care for her husband who is recovering from a stroke, told MPs at a budget committee meeting last month. “It’s probably bad for my skin.”
She says she likes to soak in a hot tub in the morning and at night to relax. “That’s my blissful time,” she said.
Her style icon status has been boosted by her black bag, dubbed a “Sanae Bag”, which is officially called the Grace Delight Tote. It is made by Hamano, a 145-year-old bagmaker based in Nagano.
Priced at 136,400 yen (US$875), the simple leather bag is just large enough to fit A4-sized papers. It is selling the best since its debut 30 years ago, according to the company.
Takaichi was carrying the bag as she walked into the prime minister’s office on October 21, and the scene immediately caused a sensation on social media.
Hamano spokesperson Takanori Kobayashi said his company is delighted to see Japan’s first female prime minister carrying the bag.
Within days, orders for the bag surged and all eight colours have sold out. The bag, which is carefully made of high-quality leather, cannot be mass-produced, and those who ordered it now have to wait until August, Kobayashi said.

Another popular item is a sparkly light-pink pen Takaichi uses to take notes; it’s Mitsubishi Pencil’s Jetstream 4&1.
The pen, the “Sanae Takaichi model”, is often out of stock at stores and internet shopping sites. Those who have found one often proudly post photos with a message: “Matching Sanae.”
The attention Takaichi is receiving is usually reserved for pop stars, athletes and influencers in Japan who fans have access to only through television or the internet.
The prime minister’s fans show their loyalty by buying the same bag and pen she uses, just as fans of star athletes like Shohei Ohtani buy replicas of his uniform to cheer on the Dodgers, experts say.
Takaichi has won admiration as a new type of role model for women who have not usually rooted for prime ministers in the past, said Namiko Kubo-Kawai, a psychology professor at Nagoya Shukutoku University.


In Japan, many female role models typically were supported for their femininity, but Takaichi is unique as a high-powered politician. Her short haircut and no-frills workwear also stand out from conventional female models.
She may not inspire many feminists, however. Takaichi is a staunch conservative who champions Japan’s traditional gender and paternalistic values. She has supported keeping the succession of Japan’s monarchy male-only. She also opposes changing a 19th-century law that would allow married couples the option of keeping separate surnames.
Still, as the first female prime minister, “she fits perfectly as a stylish role model and has won admiration from many women who have been looking for one even though they probably have never thought of rooting for a prime minister”, Kubo-Kawai said. “Female role models are becoming more diverse.”

data.title: Donald Trump looms over Fifa World Cup draw as it starts countdown to 2026 finals
data.description:
The countdown to the first 48-team Fifa World Cup begins in earnest on Friday as the draw for the 2026 finals takes place in Washington, with Donald Trump set to dominate much of the attention.
The most logistically complex World Cup in history will be held across North America from June 11 to July 19, with 16 more teams added to the global showpiece, up from the 32 nations involved in Qatar in 2022.
Friday’s ceremony, at the Kennedy Centre on the banks of the Potomac River, will be a star-studded event, but Trump will take centre stage.
Fifa president Gianni Infantino has developed a close relationship with Trump, including making several visits to the White House.

Now the US president is widely expected to be the first ever recipient of the Fifa Peace Prize, which will be awarded during the draw ceremony, “to recognise the enormous efforts of those individuals who unite people, bringing hope for future generations”, as Infantino put it.
Even the choice of draw venue is a nod to Trump, who earlier this year installed himself as chairman of the Kennedy Centre, a performing arts venue opened in the early 1970s.
Holding the draw there also means the US capital gets to play an active role in the World Cup itself having been overlooked as a match venue.
The ceremony will be co-hosted by supermodel Heidi Klum and American actor and comedian Kevin Hart, and will feature performances by Village People, Andrea Bocelli and Robbie Williams.
NFL legend Tom Brady, ice hockey icon Wayne Gretzky and former NBA superstar Shaquille O’Neal will be among the stars helping to conduct the draw.
Trump has made the World Cup a centrepiece event of both his second presidency and the 250th anniversary of US independence next year.
But he has not hesitated to inject domestic politics into the preparations, threatening to move World Cup matches from Democratic-run cities if he deems conditions to be “unsafe”.
“I would call Gianni, the head of Fifa, who’s phenomenal, and I would say, let’s move it to another location. And he would do that,” Trump said recently.
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney and Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum will also be in attendance at the ceremony.
Eleven of the 16 World Cup venues are in the United States, with three in Mexico and two in Canada.
The political element to the draw saw Iran vow to boycott the ceremony because the United States refused to grant visas to several members of its delegation.
Iranian Football Federation president Mehdi Taj denounced the US’ decision as “purely political”. Iran subsequently announced on Wednesday that it would now send representatives, including head coach Amir Ghalenoei.

Lionel Messi’s Argentina – reigning champions after winning the World Cup for the third time in Qatar in 2022 – will be among the top seeds along with European champions Spain, record five-time winners Brazil, France, Germany, England, Portugal, the Netherlands and Belgium. The three host nations complete the first pot of seeds.
The teams will be split into 12 groups of four, with the top two from each advancing alongside the eight best third-placed sides to the last 32.
For the first time, the draw will be done so as to ensure the four highest-ranked nations are kept apart. Spain, Argentina, France and England will not be able to meet before the semi-finals, if all four top their groups.

data.title: China brothers shocked that rocks used as stepping stones are 190-million-year-old dinosaur fossils
data.description:
Two brothers in southwestern China have been shocked to find that rocks they used as simple stepping stones for decades are 190-million-year-old dinosaur footprint fossils.
On November 29, researchers completed a study into dinosaur tracks found decades ago in Wuli village, Sichuan province.
The mainland media then revealed that the rocks have a fascinating backstory.

According to Guangming Daily, while quarrying stone in 1998, the Ding brothers found rocks with “chicken claw prints” and used them as stepping stones.
Wuli village, where they lived, is in Zigong city and is known as the “home of Chinese dinosaurs” in the southern Sichuan Basin.
In the 1970s and 1980s, Zigong city gained fame for the Dashanpu Middle Jurassic Dinosaur Fossil Site.

It was home to 200 dinosaur and vertebrate fossils, including rare pterosaurs and dinosaur skin. A dinosaur museum was built at the site.
In 2017, after local interest in dinosaurs grew, the daughter of one of the Ding brothers posted photographs of the flat rocks online, seeking information about their origins.
The photographs revealed sharp claw marks, round imprints and some linear traces which caught the museum’s attention.

A month later, researchers verified that the rocks were dinosaur footprint fossils.
With the Ding family’s approval, the specimens were moved to the museum for analysis.
Recently, Chinese scientists published their findings on the Wuli village fossils in the Journal of Palaeogeography.
The study focused on eight stone slabs containing 413 footprints dating back to the Early Jurassic period, around 180 million to 190 million years ago.
Scientists said they mostly belonged to Grallators and Eubrontes.
They suggest these dinosaurs used a “ground-running” gait similar to modern birds, moving at speeds of 5.8 to 8.6 km an hour.

Researchers also found rare tail drag marks on the rocks.
Associate professor Xing Lida from China University of Geosciences (Beijing) said the marks were likely caused by dinosaurs moving slowly, pausing to observe things, or to display aggressive behaviour.
The discovery has caused a buzz on mainland social media.
One online observer said: “I cannot believe the brothers had been stepping on dinosaur footprints for so many years. I am also glad the scientists uncovered these tracks to reveal the ancient history of the Earth.”
While another said: “Sichuan is not just known for spicy hotpot, it is also a hotspot for dinosaur discoveries.”

data.title: HashKey’s Hong Kong IPO highlights crypto exchange challenges under Beijing’s watchful eye
data.description:
The initial public offering (IPO) of HashKey Holdings, operator of Hong Kong’s largest cryptocurrency exchange, lays bare challenges faced by local platforms under Beijing’s watchful eye, even though the listing itself was recognition of the growing influence of digital assets in the city, according to analysts.
HashKey, established in Hong Kong in 2018, on Monday cleared its Hong Kong listing hearing after a confidential filing earlier this year, paving the way for an IPO that could raise up to US$300 million. It would be the second listed crypto exchange operator in Hong Kong, after OSL Group, a licensed virtual-asset trading platform.
The listing of HashKey comes as Hong Kong is trying to transform itself into a virtual-asset hub, even though Beijing has reiterated its hostile regulatory stance towards crypto activities on the mainland, casting a shadow over such businesses in the city.
“The acceptance of a major crypto exchange into the bourse is a strong barometer for the mainstreaming of digital assets in Hong Kong,” said Angela Ang, TRM Labs’ Asia-Pacific head of policy and strategic partnerships.

HashKey would likely debut on the Hong Kong stock exchange within the next two to three months “if it receives adequate investor interest”, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts Steven Nie and Carlton Lai said in a note on Monday.
They described HashKey as a “much more aggressive player” than its main local competitor OSL Group, which operates the OSL crypto exchange.
The trading volume on HashKey platforms, including a Hong Kong exchange for local investors and a global platform, amounted to HK$214 billion (US$27.5 billion) in the first six months, about three times that of OSL’s HK$68 billion, they noted.
HashKey’s losses for the first half of 2025, however, were significantly higher than its peer. The company reported an adjusted net loss of HK$506 million in the first six months of the year, while OSL’s losses were about HK$40 million.
“Hashkey is still making a loss while Coinbase, the biggest listed crypto exchange in the US, made over US$2 billion of profit last year,” said Alex Au, founder and chief investment officer of Alphalex Capital. “We will see how the evolving regulatory regime in Hong Kong can support the industry in expanding its service offerings and enhancing profitability.”
In 2024, HashKey’s full-year losses almost doubled to HK$1.19 billion from HK$580 million in the previous year. That reflected the “substantial upfront investments required to build and operate a licensed, compliant and scalable digital asset platform”, the company said in its IPO filing.

While HashKey targeted the Hong Kong market with “aggressive” operating expenditures, OSL was focused on overseas expansion, according to Daiwa.
That could prove a better strategy because of the “limited market size in Hong Kong”, and mainland China’s tightening restrictions on access to crypto tokens and stablecoins, Daiwa analysts said.
Beijing’s stance could hamper the potential for Hong Kong’s regulated crypto platforms, experts said. The People’s Bank of China (PBOC) last week reiterated its crackdown on crypto businesses on the mainland and singled out stablecoins for their significant risk of fraud, money laundering and illegal cross-border capital flows.
“Despite Hong Kong authorities’ clear stance that its crypto firms cannot serve Chinese users, the market has remained hopeful that this will change in the longer term,” said Ang.
“The latest statement from the PBOC is definitely a setback for the market after previous signals that Chinese authorities may be softening towards stablecoins,” she said.
Still, HashKey’s IPO was “an important event” for Hong Kong’s digital-asset ecosystem, and its revenue trajectory indicated growth potential, said Melvin Deng, CEO at digital-asset trading firm QCP Capital.
The company was in a phase of building infrastructure, boosting security and customer acquisition efforts, which, similar to most firms operating in regulated digital-asset markets, typically preceded sustained profitability, he said.

data.title: Beijing confident of Hong Kong’s ability to handle Legco election, fire aftermath
data.description:
A Beijing official in Hong Kong has voiced confidence in the city’s ability to handle both the coming Legislative Council election and the aftermath of the deadly Tai Po fire, calling local authorities’ decision to proceed with the poll respectful of the constitutional order.
Liu Guangyuan, deputy director of Beijing’s liaison office in Hong Kong, said on Thursday that the local government had adopted the “most responsible attitude” by holding the election on Sunday as scheduled, even after last week’s inferno, which claimed at least 159 lives.
“The chief executive has announced the election will be held as planned out of respect for the constitutional order,” Liu said at a forum held on China’s annual constitutional day, which falls on December 4.
“[The poll] will strongly support the recovery from the aftermath of the disaster, and [the Hong Kong government] has shown the most responsible attitude.
“We believe Hong Kong can handle both the election and disaster recovery well, and needs to cultivate solidarity and strength to move forward from grief.”

Hong Kong will elect 90 Legco members on Sunday. Among them, 20 will be directly picked by residents across 10 geographical constituencies.
Industry leaders will choose candidates to fill the 30 seats for the functional constituencies, while another 40 lawmakers will be picked by the Election Committee.
Speaking at the same forum, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said the government would undergo “systemic reforms” in the wake of the fire, adding that lawmakers would act as partners during the process.
“By electing lawmakers who have witnessed the fire alongside society, by making joint efforts with the government, by debating, promoting the review and renewal of laws, and promoting systemic reforms, we will make Hong Kong a safer place, and residents can live with assurance,” he said.
The atmosphere at Thursday’s forum was solemn, with a minute of silence being held for the victims of the fire, and the event host telling attendees to refrain from applauding speakers.
Those in attendance included Chief Secretary Eric Chan Kwok-ki, Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po, Secretary for Justice Paul Lam Ting-kwok, Chief Justice Andrew Cheung Kui-nung and Executive Council convenor Regina Ip Lau Suk-yee.

data.title: The pope visits Hong Kong for 3 hours in 1970 – from the SCMP archive
data.description:
This article was first published on December 5, 1970
By Staff reporters
A red letter day for Hong Kong Catholics
The 73-year-old pontiff, looking fit in spite of his strenuous Asian tour, responded warmly. At times he stretched out his arms in a symbol of loving embrace, at others he blessed the crowds gathered to see him.
He seemed especially pleased by the spontaneous gaiety of the greeting which 20,000 Catholic children gave him at the Happy Valley Racecourse to which he was taken by helicopter after his arrival at Kai Tak.
Message of love
In spite of the drizzle and a cold wind, he asked for the closed top of the car to be opened. He then stood up so that the children could see him.
At this, the cheers merged into a continuous, thunderous roar.
The expression of love – the main theme of the pope’s Asian tour – was also the message he conveyed to all Chinese people, including those in China, in his much-awaited farewell speech at Kai Tak Airport after the mass at the Government Stadium.
“Christ is a teacher, a shepherd and a loving redeemer for China too,” he declared.
“The Church cannot leave unsaid this good word: love, which will be forever.”
For Hong Kong, too, he had special words. In his airport broadcast he referred to the vast progress Hong Kong had made in recent years and added:
“We shall invoke God’s blessing on your continued efforts to promote justice, prosperity and peace.”

As his special aircraft took him away, His Holiness sent a message to North Vietnam expressing the wish “that the sincere efforts of the interested parties may promptly lead to the just peace so greatly desired”.
The pope was greeted at his arrival at the airport by the Roman Catholic Bishop of Hong Kong, Monsignor Francis Hsu, by the acting governor, Sir Hugh Norman-Walker, and by the colony’s leading citizens.
From there, the pope and his entourage were flown in four helicopters to the racecourse where shouts of “Viva il Papa” added to the thunderous ovation.
After his racecourse tour, the pope was driven to the Government Stadium when 50,000 people gave him another tremendous welcome.
The royal procession around the arena in an open jeep was greeted by elderly folk clapping hands entwined with rosaries, flag-waving children and teenagers uttering shrieks and screams usually accorded “pop” stars.
All were craning forward to see the pale countenance, the lifted hands and heart-warming smile. On the far side of the arena, youngsters became almost delirious with excitement.
The Holy Father became infected with this overwhelming enthusiasm and he blew kisses, made the sign of the cross and raised his arms wide as if he would enfold this multitude of Christians to his heart. This was perhaps the most moving moment.
The jeep edged past the guard of honour. Tired and cramps forgotten, the scouts and guides had their moment of glory.

And descending from the jeep, the pope went first to the purple-clad Anglican Bishop, the Right Reverend Gilbert Baker, who with Mrs Baker, received warm handshakes and cordial greetings.
Other guests of honour were met in turn: other clergy; then holders of papal honours; the disabled in wheelchairs; and members of religious orders standing four-square around the central altar.
Everywhere the pope went, Bishop Hsu was at his elbow. The 50-year-old bishop, whose appointment to head the Hong Kong diocese was made less than 18 months ago, introduced most of the dignitaries to His Holiness, called his attention and blessing to special groups, and guided him to all the right places at the right times.
The slender pontiff was escorted to the steps of the altar, where he donned green and ivory silk vestments for the celebration of the Papal High Mass.
Prayers and petitions, bilingual and reverberating around the stadium, were in progress when the rain commenced to fall steadily.
Something in the atmosphere reminded English folk present of the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II, when drenched crowds stood patiently listening to the consecration prayers relayed from Westminster Abbey.
During the offertory presentation umbrellas went up in colourful array. But the people who had heard Paul say he was happy to be with his sons and daughters in Hong Kong – “the largest Chinese diocese in the world” – stood spellbound.
The pope’s visit to Hong Kong was “all too brief” in the words of the acting governor, Norman-Walker, who welcomed him just before 1.30pm and said goodbye barely three hours later.
But those few hours were enough to give Hong Kong’s 247,000 Catholics a great spiritual uplift, for although they made up the world’s largest Chinese Catholic community, they have never before been visited by the head of their church.
The visit did much good for the colony’s church-unity movement, which is one of the world’s strongest. Catholic sources said that even before they had time to send out invitations to the papal mass, many leaders of other churches had expressed a desire to attend.
And praying with the pope and about 50 ministers and leaders of several religions was the Bishop Baker.
Bishop Baker further shored up the foundations of the Hong Kong ecumenical movement yesterday with a letter to Paul. In it, he expressed admiration for the pontiff, who had undertaken such an “arduous apostolic and missionary journey”, and said Hong Kong Anglicans were praying that, through the pope’s labours, the “message of Christ” might be more deeply understood by the people of the Far East.
Strenuous
It was a strenuous three hours for the 73-year-old pontiff. But throughout, he looked well and walked with a spry step. Between his flowing white robes and white skullcap, his face looked sun-tanned and had a warm glow of health.
Paul usually speaks softly and slowly. But yesterday while preaching to just over 50,000 people in the Government Stadium, his amplified voice sounded very tired, almost weak, and he faltered over many of the English words that don’t come easily to him. There were however, three words that he pronounced with heavy emphasis, every syllable distinct.
The words came at the end of a sentence, at the end of his sermon: “Christ is a teacher, a shepherd and a loving redeemer FOR CHINA TOO.”
His Holiness habitually emphasises the last words of a sentence, but this time he was so forceful – far more so than in any other part of his sermon – that it was clear he wanted his message of love and peace to reach Chinese everywhere.
That however, was virtually all he said about China. He had decided to restrain his public expressions of love and concern for the Chinese people, although Catholic sources said he had privately compared his 1970 Far East mission to that of Francis Xavier, the missionary who died on the island of Shangchuan in 1552 while trying to get into China.

data.title: Chinese families ‘hope truth will come out’ when hunt for missing flight MH370 resumes
data.description:
Malaysia’s announcement that the search for missing Malaysia Airlines flight 370 will resume was welcome news for Jiang Hui, whose mother was a passenger on the plane.
“I hope the truth will come out soon, the wait has been such a torment for the families,” he wrote in a social media post sharing the news.
Malaysia’s transport ministry made the announcement on Wednesday, more than a decade after the Boeing 777 carrying 227 passengers and 12 crew vanished en route from Kuala Lumpur to Beijing in one of aviation’s biggest mysteries.
Malaysian authorities said the search – which began in March in the southern Indian Ocean but was suspended after a few weeks due to poor weather – would resume on December 30.
It is being led by US-based exploration firm Ocean Infinity under a “no find, no fee” arrangement.


“The families welcome the continued search from the Malaysian government and Ocean Infinity,” said Jiang, whose mother Jiang Cuiyun was one of more than 150 Chinese passengers on the flight.
“We hope the search on reward can be changed into an open and long-term one, with no fixed end date. The families would be willing to participate in such a reward search.”
He also said he hoped more Chinese could join the search.
The announcement has renewed hopes among relatives like Jiang that they might finally get some closure.
Li Eryou, a farmer in Hubei province whose son was on the flight, wrote on social media that “the authorities need to tell the families – after 11 troubling years – what exactly happened”.
“When the 2018 safety report said investigators could not be sure of the real reason that MH370 went missing it was an insult for all the families and those who were following this,” he added.
Two-thirds of the passengers on the flight were Chinese. There were also Malaysian, French, Australian, Indonesian, Indian, American, Ukrainian and Canadian nationals, among others.
After the plane disappeared without trace in 2014, the Chinese families formed advocacy groups to push for the search to continue and negotiate with the Chinese and Malaysian governments.
Over the past 11 years, relatives have shown up at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Beijing every week to ask for updates and to call for more to be done to find the wreckage of the plane.
They also regularly visit temples in the Chinese capital to pray for their lost family members.


Jiang, who is the organiser of a WeChat group for relatives, has taken part in efforts to find the plane, including a 2016 trip to Madagascar organised by support group Voice 370 to search for plane debris.
He said he was trying to push the search forward in three ways – through public opinion, legal action and diplomatic pressure.
A Beijing court began hearing compensation claims from the families in 2023. Jiang and about 40 other families are suing Malaysia Airlines, its insurer, Boeing and the manufacturer of the plane’s engine.
They are seeking compensation, an apology, psychological assistance and funding to continue the search for the plane.
“To be optimistic, we might see results by the end of this year,” Jiang said.
Others are using social media to share news on aviation safety or any new findings that could be relevant to the plane’s whereabouts.
Li, the farmer, has written some 3,000 poems for his missing son, which he has also posted on social media.
“Let’s meet again, my child, when the frost of spring still hangs, when the wind has not yet blown away the leaves, when my aged arms and legs can still move,” he wrote in February.

data.title: Drink in Focus: Bancha & Chestnut Alexander at Madara
data.description:
At the end of a long year, a comforting cocktail and human connection are always in order, especially during the colder winter months. While most might seek a hot toddy, an Irish coffee or even festive eggnog, the velvety, rich profile of the classic brandy Alexander – combining cognac, crème de cacao and cream – is an underrated option for something sweet and satisfying.
Few places riff on the brandy Alexander quite like Madara, an extension of modern izakaya Yorucho in Causeway Bay. Co-founder Rayven Leung and his team build on the success of Leung’s other venue, Takumi Mixology Salon, by focusing on bespoke cocktails alongside signatures focused on tea, fruits and – more unusually – Shakespeare. The first ingredient was the starting point for the Bancha & Chestnut Alexander.
Leung, who usually prefers to limit the number of ingredients in a cocktail, pushes himself to his maximum of six for this drink: roasted bancha green tea leaves, cognac, a cask-aged mugi (barley) shochu, adzuki liqueur, Sakari matcha liqueur, and cream.

The observant may spot the glaring omission of chestnut as an ingredient. “Hongkongers,” Leung explains, “rarely order cocktails [like the brandy Alexander] that may be too sweet or creamy because they might be heavy. We changed the name so guests can focus on the flavour profile – it allows us to share how we can use bancha and mugi shochu to evoke the flavour of charcoal-roasted chestnuts without actually including them in the cocktail.”
Everything about the cocktail – from ingredients and assembly to presentation and serving vessel – is tailored to evoke charcoal-roasted chestnuts, that local winter treat sold from street carts.
“Bancha has the exact same natural fragrance [as] fresh chestnuts,” Leung notes, adding that the leaves also have “that charcoal character, which is different from, say, chestnut paste on cakes or chestnut extracts or syrups”.
Bancha green tea leaves are harvested at a more mature stage than sencha, and thus have a heartier, earthier flavour. The leaves are roasted in front of the guest to release their aroma during assembly as part of the presentation.

“The fragrance is key because it helps the guest mentally hone in on the flavour in focus,” Leung explains. “There’s a fresh chestnut fragrance and flavour that bancha and cask-aged mugi shochu pair well to reproduce. In Japan, this is a common approach, but it’s not often seen in Hong Kong. The cask maturation of the mugi shochu adds depth and increases the complexity of that fresh chestnut flavour.”
Cognac is added partly due to its use in the classic brandy Alexander, but also to supplement the alcohol content of the shochu and round out the flavour of the bancha. Matcha and adzuki liqueurs, along with the cream, help refine and lengthen the sweet finish of the drink.
“There are a lot of details that affect the final result and experience of a cocktail,” Leung notes. “It comes down to whether a mixologist can control these things, and if they understand why they use certain shaking methods, serving temperatures and so on.”

Leung has partnered with local ceramic gallery and studio Waka Artisans to use their ceramic tea cups as serving vessels. “Our main concern is how the guest drinks from a cup and how ingredients linger, or don’t linger, when sipping,” he says. “These ceramic cups are quite wide, which allows a drink to sweep over the whole palate – great for drinks with complex flavour profiles. What the guest might end up doing is licking their lips when finishing a sip – something that suits tea.”
The Bancha & Chestnut Alexander is actually the result of a guest’s bespoke request – an aspect key to the sense of comfort the drink brings.
“Whenever we make a cocktail, I aim to understand what the guest really needs – whether they are cold or warm, happy or sad; if they need company or solace,” says Leung. “What makes me happiest is when guests give us a unique challenge or prompt. This [pushes] us to think creatively and try things we’ve never tried before. If you share the challenge of creativity with the guest, you’ll open up new possibilities. We treat that type of trust very seriously.”
Anatomy of a cocktail
Name: Bancha & Chestnut Alexander
Bar: Madara, 16/F, Circle Tower, 28 Tang Lung Street, Causeway Bay
Price: HK$185
Main ingredients: The Hachi sherry-cask-matured mugi shochu, bancha tea leaves, cognac, Sakari matcha liqueur, adzuki liqueur, cream
Presentation: Served in a ceramic Waka Artisans cup, garnished with orange and lime peel
Flavours: Earthy, sweet, fragrant, herbaceous, nutty, creamy
